8.1 Introduction
You have already studied about triangles, and in particular, right triangles, in your
earlier classes. Let us take some examples from our surroundings where right triangles
can be imagined to be formed. For instance :
1. Suppose the students of a school are
visiting Qutub Minar. Now, if a student
is looking at the top of the Minar, a right
triangle can be imagined to be made,
as shown in Fig 8.1. Can the student
find out the height of the Minar, without
actually measuring it?
    2.  Suppose a girl is sitting on the balcony
of her house located on the bank of a
river.  She is looking down at a flower
pot placed on a stair of a temple situated
nearby on the other bank of the river.
A right triangle is imagined to be made
in this situation as shown in Fig.8.2. If
you know the height at which the
person is sitting, can you find the width
of the river?

8.1 Introduction
You have already studied about triangles, and in particular, right triangles, in your
earlier classes. Let us take some examples from our surroundings where right triangles
can be imagined to be formed. For instance :
1. Suppose the students of a school are
visiting Qutub Minar. Now, if a student
is looking at the top of the Minar, a right
triangle can be imagined to be made,
as shown in Fig 8.1. Can the student
find out the height of the Minar, without
actually measuring it?
    2.  Suppose a girl is sitting on the balcony
of her house located on the bank of a
river.  She is looking down at a flower
pot placed on a stair of a temple situated
nearby on the other bank of the river.
A right triangle is imagined to be made
in this situation as shown in Fig.8.2. If
you know the height at which the
person is sitting, can you find the width
of the river?
INTRODUCTION TO
TRIGONOMETRY
Fig. 8.1
Fig. 8.2
Reprint 2026-27

114
MATHEMATICS
3. Suppose a hot air balloon is flying in
the air.  A girl happens to spot the
balloon in the sky and runs to her
mother to tell her about it.  Her mother
rushes out of the house to look at the
balloon.Now when the girl had spotted
the balloon intially it was at point A.
When both the mother and daughter
came out to see it, it had already
travelled to another point B. Can you
find the altitude of B from the ground?
In all the situations given above, the distances or heights can be found by using
some mathematical techniques, which come under a branch of mathematics called
вҖҳtrigonometryвҖҷ. The word вҖҳtrigonometryвҖҷ is derived from the Greek words вҖҳtriвҖҷ
(meaning three), вҖҳgonвҖҷ (meaning sides) and вҖҳmetronвҖҷ (meaning measure). In fact,
trigonometry is the study of relationships between the sides and angles of a triangle.
The earliest known work on trigonometry was recorded in Egypt and Babylon. Early
astronomers used it to find out the distances of the stars and planets from the Earth.
Even today, most of the technologically advanced methods used in Engineering and
Physical Sciences are based on trigonometrical concepts.
In this chapter, we will study some ratios of the sides of a right triangle with
respect to its acute angles, called trigonometric ratios of the angle. We will restrict
our discussion to acute angles only. However, these  ratios can be extended to other
angles also. We will also define the trigonometric ratios for angles of measure 0 and
90. We will calculate trigonometric ratios for some specific angles and establish
some identities involving these ratios, called trigonometric identities.

8.2 Trigonometric Ratios
In Section 8.1, you have seen some right triangles
imagined to be formed in different situations.
Let us take a right triangle ABC as shown
in Fig. 8.4.
Here, пғҗ CAB (or, in brief, angle A) is an
acute angle. Note the position of the side BC
with respect to angle A. It faces пғҗ A. We call it
the side opposite to angle A. AC is the
hypotenuse of the right triangle and the side AB
is a part of пғҗ A. So, we call it the side
adjacent to angle A.

The first use of the idea of вҖҳsineвҖҷ in the way we use
it today was in the work Aryabhatiyam by Aryabhata,
in A.D. 500. Aryabhata used the word ardha-jya
for the half-chord, which was shortened to jya or
jiva in due course. When the Aryabhatiyam was
translated into Arabic, the word jiva was retained as
it is. The word jiva was translated into sinus, which
means curve, when the Arabic version was translated
into Latin. Soon the word sinus, also used as sine,
became common in mathematical texts throughout
Europe. An English Professor of astronomy Edmund
Gunter (1581вҖ“1626), first used the abbreviated
notation вҖҳsinвҖҷ.
The origin of the terms вҖҳcosineвҖҷ and вҖҳtangentвҖҷ was much later. The cosine function
arose from the need to compute the sine of the complementary angle. Aryabhatta
called it kotijya. The name cosinus originated with Edmund Gunter. In 1674, the
English Mathematician Sir Jonas Moore first used the abbreviated notation вҖҳcosвҖҷ.
Remark : Note that the symbol sin A is used as an
abbreviation for вҖҳthe sine of the angle AвҖҷ. sin A is not
the product of вҖҳsinвҖҷ and A. вҖҳsinвҖҷ separated from A
has no meaning. Similarly, cos A is not the product of
вҖҳcosвҖҷ and A. Similar interpretations follow for other
trigonometric ratios also.

Let us see what happens to the trigonometric ratios of angle
A, if it is made smaller and smaller in the right triangle ABC
(see Fig. 8.16), till it becomes zero. As пғҗ A gets smaller and
smaller, the length of the side BC decreases.The point C gets
closer to point B, and finally when пғҗ A becomes very close
to 0, AC becomes almost the same as AB (see Fig. 8.17).

Now, let us see what happens to the trigonometric ratios of пғҗ A, when it is made
larger and larger in пҒ„ ABC till it becomes 90. As пғҗ A gets larger and larger, пғҗ C gets
smaller and smaller. Therefore, as in the case above, the length of the side AB goes on
decreasing. The point A gets closer to point B. Finally when пғҗ A is very close to 90,
пғҗ C becomes very close to 0 and the side AC almost coincides with side BC
(see Fig. 8.18).

When пғҗ C is very close to 0, пғҗ A is very close to 90, side AC is nearly the
same as side BC, and so sin A is very close to 1. Also when пғҗ A is very close to 90,
пғҗ C is very close to 0, and the side AB is nearly zero, so cos A is very close to 0.
So, we define :
sin 90 = 1 and cos 90 = 0.
Now, why donвҖҷt you find the other trigonometric ratios of 90?
We shall now give the values of all the trigonometric ratios of 0, 30, 45, 60
and 90 in Table 8.1, for ready reference.

3 Trigonometric Ratios of Some Specific Angles From geometry, you are already familiar with the construction of angles of 30, 45, 60 and 90. In this section, we will find the values of the trigonometric ratios for these angles and, of course, for 0. Fig. 8.13 Reprint 2026-27 122 MATHEMATICS Trigonometric Ratios of 45 In пҒ„ ABC, right-angled at B, if one angle is 45, then the other angle is also 45, i.e., пғҗ A = пғҗ C = 45 (see Fig. 8.14). So, BC = AB (Why?) Now, Suppose BC = AB = a. Then by Pythagoras Theorem, AC2 = AB2 + BC2 = a2 + a2 = 2a2, and, therefore, AC = 2 a пғ— Using the definitions of the trigonometric ratios, we have : sin 45 = side opposite to angle 45 BC 1 hypotenuse AC 2 2 a a пҖҪ пҖҪ пҖҪ cos 45 = side adjacent toangle 45 AB 1 hypotenuse AC 2 2 a a пҖҪ пҖҪ пҖҪ tan 45 = side opposite to angle 45 BC 1 side adjacent to angle 45 AB a a пҖҪ пҖҪ пҖҪ Also, cosec 45 = 1 2 sin 45 пҖҪ пӮ° , sec 45 = 1 2 cos 45 пҖҪ пӮ° , cot 45 = 1 1 tan 45 пҖҪ пӮ° . Trigonometric Ratios of 30 and 60 Let us now calculate the trigonometric ratios of 30 and 60. Consider an equilateral triangle ABC. Since each angle in an equilateral triangle is 60, therefore, пғҗ A = пғҗ B = пғҗ C = 60. Draw the perpendicular AD from A to the side BC (see Fig. 8.15). Now пҒ„ ABD пҒҖпҒ„ ACD (Why?) Therefore, BD = DC and пғҗ BAD = пғҗ CAD (CPCT) Now observe that: пҒ„ ABD is a right triangle, right-angled at D with пғҗ BAD = 30 and пғҗ ABD = 60 (see Fig. 8.15). Fig. 8.15 Fig. 8.14 Reprint 2026-27 INTRODUCTION TO TRIGONOMETRY 123 As you know, for finding the trigonometric ratios, we need to know the lengths of the sides of the triangle. So, let us suppose that AB = 2a. Then, BD = 1 BC = 2 a and AD2 = AB2 вҖ“ BD2 = (2a)2 вҖ“ (a)2 = 3a2, Therefore, AD = 3 a Now, we have : sin 30 = BD 1 AB 2 2 a a пҖҪ пҖҪ , cos 30 = AD 3 3 AB 2 2 a a пҖҪ пҖҪ tan 30 = BD 1 AD 3 3 a a пҖҪ пҖҪ . Also, cosec 30 = 1 2, sin 30 пҖҪ пӮ° sec 30 = 1 2 cos 30 3 пҖҪ пӮ° cot 30 = 1 3 tan 30 пҖҪ пӮ° . Similarly, sin 60 = AD 3 3 AB 2 2 a a пҖҪ пҖҪ , cos 60 = 1 2 , tan 60 = 3 , cosec 60 = 2 , 3 sec 60 = 2 and cot 60 = 1 3 пғ— Trigonometric Ratios of 0 and 90 Let us see what happens to the trigonometric ratios of angle A, if it is made smaller and smaller in the right triangle ABC (see Fig. 8.16), till it becomes zero. As пғҗ A gets smaller and smaller, the length of the side BC decreases.The point C gets closer to point B, and finally when пғҗ A becomes very close to 0, AC becomes almost the same as AB (see Fig. 8.17). Fig. 8.17 Fig. 8.16 Reprint 2026-27 124 MATHEMATICS When пғҗ A is very close to 0, BC gets very close to 0 and so the value of sin A = BC AC is very close to 0. Also, when пғҗ A is very close to 0, AC is nearly the same as AB and so the value of cos A = AB AC is very close to 1. This helps us to see how we can define the values of sin A and cos A when A = 0. We define : sin 0 = 0 and cos 0 = 1. Using these, we have : tan 0 = sin 0 cos 0 = 0, cot 0 = 1 , tan 0 which is not defined. (Why?) sec 0 = 1 cos 0пӮ° = 1 and cosec 0 = 1 , sin 0пӮ° which is again not defined.(Why?) Now, let us see what happens to the trigonometric ratios of пғҗ A, when it is made larger and larger in пҒ„ ABC till it becomes 90. As пғҗ A gets larger and larger, пғҗ C gets smaller and smaller. Therefore, as in the case above, the length of the side AB goes on decreasing. The point A gets closer to point B. Finally when пғҗ A is very close to 90, пғҗ C becomes very close to 0 and the side AC almost coincides with side BC (see Fig. 8.18). Fig. 8.18 When пғҗ C is very close to 0, пғҗ A is very close to 90, side AC is nearly the same as side BC, and so sin A is very close to 1. Also when пғҗ A is very close to 90, пғҗ C is very close to 0, and the side AB is nearly zero, so cos A is very close to 0. So, we define : sin 90 = 1 and cos 90 = 0. Now, why donвҖҷt you find the other trigonometric ratios of 90? We shall now give the values of all the trigonometric ratios of 0, 30, 45, 60 and 90 in Table 8.1, for ready reference. 4 Trigonometric Identities You may recall that an equation is called an identity when it is true for all values of the variables involved. Similarly, an equation involving trigonometric ratios of an angle is called a trigonometric identity, if it is true for all values of the angle(s) involved. In this section, we will prove one trigonometric identity, and use it further to prove other useful trigonometric identities. In пҒ„ ABC, right-angled at B (see Fig. 8.21), we have: AB2 + BC2 = AC2 (1) Dividing each term of (1) by AC2, we get 2 2 2 2 AB BC AC AC пҖ« = 2 2 AC AC i.e., 2 2 AB BC AC AC пғҰ пғ¶ пғҰ пғ¶ пҖ« пғ§ пғ· пғ§ пғ· пғЁ пғё пғЁ пғё = 2 AC AC пғҰ пғ¶ пғ§ пғ· пғЁ пғё i.e., (cos A)2 + (sin A)2 = 1 i.e., cos2 A + sin2 A = 1 (2) This is true for all A such that 0 пӮЈ A пӮЈ 90. So, this is a trigonometric identity. Let us now divide (1) by AB2. We get 2 2 2 2 AB BC AB AB пҖ« = 2 2 AC AB or, 2 2 AB BC AB AB пғҰ пғ¶ пғҰ пғ¶ пҖ« пғ§ пғ· пғ§ пғ· пғЁ пғё пғЁ пғё = 2 AC AB пғҰ пғ¶ пғ§ пғ· пғЁ пғё i.e., 1 + tan2 A = sec2 A (3) Is this equation true for A = 0? Yes, it is. What about A = 90? Well, tan A and sec A are not defined for A = 90. So, (3) is true for all A such that 0 пӮЈ A пҖј 90. Let us see what we get on dividing (1) by BC2. We get 2 2 2 2 AB BC BC BC пҖ« = 2 2 AC BC Fig. 8.21 Reprint 2026-27 INTRODUCTION TO TRIGONOMETRY 129 i.e., 2 2 AB BC BC BC пғҰ пғ¶ пғҰ пғ¶ пҖ« пғ§ пғ· пғ§ пғ· пғЁ пғё пғЁ пғё = 2 AC BC пғҰ пғ¶ пғ§ пғ· пғЁ пғё i.e., cot2 A + 1 = cosec2 A (4) Note that cosec A and cot A are not defined for A = 0. Therefore (4) is true for all A such that 0 < A пӮЈ 90. Using these identities, we can express each trigonometric ratio in terms of other trigonometric ratios, i.e., if any one of the ratios is known, we can also determine the values of other trigonometric ratios. Let us see how we can do this using these identities. Suppose we know that tan A = 1 3 пғ— Then, cot A = 3 . Since, sec2 A = 1 + tan2 A = 1 4, 1 3 3 пҖ« пҖҪ sec A = 2 3 , and cos A = 3 2 пғ— Again, sin A = 2 3 1 1 cos A 1 4 2 пҖӯ пҖҪ пҖӯ пҖҪ . Therefore, cosec A = 2.
